data.title: MKS to sell chemicals unit in a push for chip business: FT
data.description:
MKS (NASDAQ:MKSI), a supplier to the semiconductor industry, is in the final stages of offloading its specialty chemicals division worth $1B to double down on supplying chipmakers, The Financial Times reported on Saturday.  

The Andover, Massachusetts-based company, which counts Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) among its suppliers, has engaged advisors to divest the division, and the sales process has reached an advanced stage, the FT reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The company has attracted multiple strategic buyers and private equity groups in the auction, they said, adding that there is no certainty about a final deal.  

Having joined MIKS (NASDAQ:MKSI) in 2021 following its $5.1B buyout of Atotech, the division has generated roughly $100M in adjusted earnings annually, focusing on manufacturing technologies for advanced surface modification, among other functions.

The company is expected to retain a section of the division, which manufactures equipment used in the production of semiconductors and circuit boards.

In 2024, MKSI’s Semiconductor division generated $1.5B in revenue with ~1% YoY growth, and revenue from its Electronics and Packaging unit held steady at $922M while its Speciality Industrial unit contracted ~5% with $1.2B in revenue.

data.title: BITF, CLSK, JEF, ABTC, WAL among week's double-digit movers: financials wrap
data.description:
This week's biggest movers within the financials space predominantly featured Argentine lenders, crypto-related companies, and U.S.-based financial firms. Stocks reversed lower late in the week after President Donald Trump said the U.S. will impose a 100% tariff on China.

The XLF Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) finished the week ended Oct. 10 down 2.9%, compared with the broader S&P 500 Index's 2.4% slide.

The biggest loser was Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), tumbling 19% in the week, after the investment banking and capital markets firm disclosed that it has around $161M of exposure to First Brands, an auto parts maker that filed for bankruptcy last month;

American Bitcoin (ABTC) dropped 15% as the price of bitcoin (BTC-USD) suffered steep selling pressure after the China tariff news drove a broader risk-off tone;

Western Alliance Bancorp (WAL), which also faces exposure to First Brands through a leveraged facility with a fund tied to Jefferies (JEF), fell 14%;

Neptune Insurance Holdings (NP), the flood insurer that went public early this month, shed 13%; and

Main Street Capital (MAIN), which during the week shared its private loan portfolio activity for Q3, was down 12.5%.

On the winning side, Bitfarms (BITF) +40%, Cleanspark (CLSK) +21% and IREN (IREN) +19% all surged even with lower crypto prices; and

Argentine banks Banco Macro (BMA) +18% and Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) +16% jumped after the U.S. Treasury finalized a $20B currency swap framework with Argentina and directly purchased Argentine pesos.

data.title: Mass firings underway at CDC as shutdown-related layoffs continue
data.description:
Dozens of workers at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), including top-ranking scientists, have received layoff notices as the Trump administration begins its latest round of job terminations across multiple federal agencies amid an ongoing government shutdown.

According to the New York Times, the affected C.D.C. employees were informed of their job losses via email just before 9 p.m. ET on Friday. Citing a person familiar with the matter, the paper added that 70 Epidemic Intelligence Service officers have also received layoff notices.

Workers running the CDC’s Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report were also being let go, the NYT reported, adding that others to face job losses included scientists, including those leading the CDC offices tasked with addressing respiratory disorders and global health.

Citing a current CDC official and a former administration official with direct knowledge of the matter, Politico reported that the CDC’s entire Washington office is facing terminations. Layoffs are also affecting its Global Health Center and the National Center for Immunization and Respiratory Diseases.

“I don’t have a count, but it’s a lot, and the notices are still coming,” a CDC official said on the condition of anonymity. “Entire offices are being eliminated, including people in senior leadership roles.”

The report came after a court filing from the Office of Management and Budget indicated that more than 4,000 workers across seven federal agencies have received reduction-in-force (RIF) notices as the government shutdown enters the 10th day.

The layoffs affected about 1,100 and 1,200 employees at the Health and Human Services, according to the court filing submitted on Friday in response to a lawsuit from the American Federation of Government Employees and the AFL-CIO.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

  • IRS furloughs almost half its workforce as shutdown drags on

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Netflix, AMD and Micron among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the red on Friday, as U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to hike tariffs on Chinese goods.

Nasdaq (COMP:IND) rose 0.4% during the week, while Dow (DJI) fell 0.7%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Seaport bullish on Netflix’s ad revenue, engagement optimism

Seaport upgraded Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) to Buy from Neutral and increased the ad revenue estimates for the streaming giant to more closely track the TV viewership share.

"We think advertising could double to $3.1B this year and could grow at 48% annually through 2030E to reach ~$16B. Channel checks indicate same-store ad buying could be tracking better than +16% for 3Q25, but remember that a number of markets have been layered on in the past year," Seaport said.

The brokerage also established a new price target of $1,385 on the stock.

The analysts also noted that engagement on the Netflix platform continues to be strong, with at least a couple of 'cultural zeitgeist' titles driving viewership share and possibly subscriber net addition gains.

AMD upgraded following megadeal with OpenAI

Jefferies upgraded Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) to Buy from Hold following the massive infrastructure deal the semiconductor firm reached with Microsoft-backed (MSFT) OpenAI (OPENAI).

"The partnership has the potential to generate well over $100B from OpenAI, new, and existing customers over the next four years," according to Jefferies analysts, led by Blayne Curtis, and increased its price target to $300 from $170.

Piper Sandler also reiterated its Overweight rating on AMD and increased its PT to $240 from $190, saying the deal has the potential to provide extended benefits to AMD.

"Revenues from this deal are expected to begin in the second half of 2026 with significant revenue coming in 2027," the analysts noted, adding that the brokerage expects 3Q26 to be modest with a larger ramp in 4Q26 and a full ramp through 2027.

Micron gets rating upgrade at Morgan Stanley

Morgan Stanley upgraded Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) to Overweight from Equal-weight and raised PT to $220 from $160 on the stock.

"Micron is pushing the envelope on valuation as the group rallies, but we believe we are looking at multiple quarters of double digit price increases which can lead to substantially higher earnings power - and resolve any lingering questions on specialty high bandwidth memory for AI," said analysts led by Joseph Moore.

The analysts noted that momentum on core DRAM pricing continues to surprise them, at the same time, near-term worries on High Bandwidth Memory, or HBM, are being solved by DRAM economic improvement. Moore and his team also said concerns about specialty memory for AI — HBM — are easing, as the stronger DRAM backdrop helps negotiations for HBM3e.

Abercrombie & Fitch downgraded on same store sales decline

JP Morgan cut Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF) to Neutral from Overweight, saying it sees Q3 revenue growth and same store sales (SSS) coming in below Street consensus.

Analyst Matthew Boss sees Q3 revenue growth of 3.9% year over year, which compares to the Street's estimate of 6.2% and management's projection of 5%-7%. The bank also lowered its PT to $103 from $145.

Boss noted that fieldwork indicates there is a "hangover" effect due to first half of the year's large promotion and clearance activity, which is negatively impacting customer conversion rates as well as regular-priced selling.

KeyBanc upped its price target on Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) to $240 from $220, though it also expressed China-related caution. The brokerage said it hiked PT to reflect its incrementally optimistic view of AMAT’s ability to benefit from, and help drive, the boom in AI-related investment. However, the firm now expects Applied Materials to earn $9.37 per share in fiscal 2025, down from a prior view of $9.40, and in-line with the consensus. The firm also cut its estimates for fiscal 2026 and 2027 to $9.67 and $10.91, from $9.90 and $11.06, respectively.

Wells Fargo downgraded Emerson (NYSE:EMR) to Equal Weight from Overweight with a $140 PT, trimmed from $150, as the brokerage believes the company is "a better business than valuation gives it credit for," but lacks near-term catalysts with downsized FY 2025 exit rate growth and tough H1 2026 margin comps.

Wells Fargo resumed coverage of Walt Disney (NYSE:DIS) with an Overweight rating, pointing to maturation and growth across its assets that create more predictable earnings growth. Wells Fargo has an PT of $159 on the stock.

Plug Power (NASDAQ:PLUG) was cut to Hold from Buy by Clear Street, with analysts citing stretched valuation following a sharp run-up in the stock.

data.title: Layoff notices sent to thousands of federal workers as shutdown drags on
data.description:
The Trump administration has sent layoff notices to thousands of federal employees across multiple agencies as the government shutdown reaches the 10th day, with Republicans and Democrats failing to break a budget impasse.

According to a court filing on Friday, the Office of Management and Budget has said more than 4,000 federal workers across seven government agencies have received "reduction in force" (RIF) notices.

The OMB submitted the filing in response to a court case initiated by two major unions, the American Federation of Government Employees and the AFL-CIO, over the shutdown-related layoffs at a federal court in Northern California.

According to the filing, workers at the U.S. Departments of Treasury, Health and Human Services, and Education were the worst affected, contributing more than 50% of RIF notices. Other agencies facing layoffs include the departments of Homeland Security, Energy, Housing and Urban Development, and the Environmental Protection Agency.

The U.S. government began shutting down on Oct. 1 after Democrats refused to support Republicans’ budget plans in a disagreement essentially over the Trump administration’s curbs on healthcare spending.

This week, U.S. stocks witnessed the worst decline since late May as the S&P (SP500) fell 2.4%, while the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) dropped 2.5% and 2.7%, respectively, as Trump’s latest tariff threats against China added to worries over the shutdown.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on the Government Shutdown

data.title: Trending stocks this week: Wall Street tumbles as trade war fears resurface
data.description:
Wall Street ended its toughest week since late May, as U.S. markets tumbled on Friday amid renewed trade war fears. The sharp sell-off erased earlier gains and turned what had been a positive week into a significant loss for equities.

For the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) fell 2.4%, the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) dropped 2.5%, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) slid 2.7%.

The S&P 500 (SP500) had reached another record high on Wednesday, with investors largely ignoring the ongoing U.S. government shutdown and a lull in economic data. Meanwhile, precious metals stole the spotlight, with spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) breaking above $4,000/oz and spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) topping $50/oz for the first time ever.

Friday’s reversal came after President Donald Trump accused China of imposing “hostile” export controls on rare earths and announced a 100% tariff on Chinese products. The remarks rattled markets, sending the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) to their worst single-day declines since early April.

Here's a list of stocks that caught investor attention this week:

Serve Robotics (SERV) surged after entering a multi-year strategic partnership with DoorDash (DASH) to roll out autonomous robot deliveries across the U.S. The company also announced a stock offering, aiming to raise gross proceeds of approximately $100M.

Applied Digital (APLD) jumped after its first quarter results topped Wall Street expectations. First quarter revenue rose 84% year over year to total $64.2M, which exceeded the consensus estimate of $45.46M. It also reported adjusted losses per share of $0.03, which was much better than the estimate calling for losses per share of $0.16.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) signed a pact with Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI (OPENAI) to roll out AI infrastructure under a deal that is expected to deliver tens of billions of dollars in revenue for AMD. The deal is among the latest being carried out by OpenAI to build out more computing capacity amid the AI boom.

AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) struck a commercial agreement with U.S. telecom Verizon VZ) to provide space-based cellular and broadband services to Verizon customers starting in 2026. ASTS shares have nearly quadrupled year to date, dwarfing the 15% rally of the benchmark S&P index for the same period.

IREN (IREN) jumped as it announced additional multi-year cloud services contracts with AI companies for Nvidia Blackwell GPU deployments. In addition, the company announced an offering of $875 million aggregate principal amount of convertible senior notes due 2031.

Tesla (TSLA) announced a new version of its Model Y, which is meant to make the EV more competitive on price since the expiration of the electric vehicle tax credit. The EV maker priced the new Model Y version at $39,990, which is about 15% cheaper than the base version. In addition, TSLA unveiled a new price for its standard rear-wheel drive Model 3 of $36,990.

Archer Aviation (ACHR) had a volatile week, fueled by speculation it would be part of Tesla (TSLA) product-reveal day. However, the stock then sank 8.5% when it was not included in TSLA’s announcement.

Ferrari N.V. (RACE) revealed new details about its first electric vehicle, currently codenamed Elettrica. The upcoming EV will feature in-house–designed electric motors delivering more than 1,000 horsepower, enabling acceleration from 0 to 62 mph in just 2.5 seconds. Powered by a 122 kWh battery pack, the Elettrica will offer an estimated range of over 323 miles and reach a top speed of 192 mph. Separately, as part of its Capital Markets Day presentation, Ferrari (RACE) lowered its forecast for electric vehicles to account for 20% of its lineup by 2030, down from a prior target of 40%.

UiPath (PATH) shares surged 18.8% on Thursday to reach a 16-month high. The software and automation company is benefiting from growing excitement around agentic artificial intelligence, following announcements of new partnerships with major AI players, including OpenAI.

PepsiCo (PEP) shares advanced after the company reported better-than-expected third-quarter earnings and announced the appointment of a new chief financial officer. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $2.29, beating consensus estimates by $0.03, while revenue rose 2.7% year-over-year to $23.9 billion. However, organic sales increased 1.3%, falling short of the expected 2.1% growth.

data.title: Trump’s 100% tariff threat on China leads to over $19B in crypto liquidations
data.description:
President Donald Trump’s warning to impose 100% additional tariffs on goods imported from China precipitated a record sell-off in crypto assets on Friday, triggering more than $19B in liquidations from over 1.6M traders over the past 24 hours.

Crypto markets were spooked by Trump’s latest tariff threat, which he initially announced through a social media post on Friday, leading to what data tracker CoinGlass identified as “the largest liquidation event in crypto history.”

Later in the day, exacerbating the crypto sell-off, he confirmed plans to impose 100% additional tariffs on goods imported from China starting on Nov. 1, along with export controls on Chinese-made software.

Only days after reaching an all-time high earlier this week, Bitcoin (BTC-USD), the first-ever cryptocurrency, was hovering around $112.0K at 8:10 a.m. ET on Saturday, indicating an ~8% drop over the past 24 hours.

Ether (ETH-USD) has slipped 12% to $3.8K, followed by ~17%, ~12%, and ~23% declines in Solana (SOL-USD), Ripple (XRP-USD), and Dogecoin (DOGE-USD), which have plummeted to $182.68, $2.47, and $0.19, respectively.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) suffered the bulk of the damage, with $5.4B and $4.5B in liquidations over the past 24 hours, respectively, followed by $2.02B in Solana (SOL-USD) and $709.9M in Ripple (XRP-USD).

data.title: NI Holdings appoints Cindy Launer as CEO
data.description:
• NI Holdings (NASDAQ:NODK) on Friday said that Seth Daggett has stepped down as President and CEO, effective immediately, to pursue other opportunities.

  • The company’s Board has appointed Cindy Launer, former Interim CEO in 2024, as the new President and CEO.
  • Launer, who has 18 years of experience in the insurance industry, including a role as COO at AIG, will also remain on NI Holdings’ Board.
  • The Board expressed confidence in her ability to lead the company through its next phase of growth.
  • Source: Press Release
